def: The set of reasons and their arrangement used to support a claim.
A statement taken as true and used as the basis of an argument.
A judgement reached by reasoning from premises.
To present and explain something clearly and in full.
To consider a question carefully from various sides.
Tightly reasoned and consistent throughout.
A proof or record that supports a claim.
The scholarly assessment of how reliable a source is.
To give something a firm basis in evidence or reason.
To show clearly, with evidence, that something is true.
A figure of speech that describes one thing as if it were another.
The expression of meaning through language that signifies the opposite.
A statement that makes something seem greater than it is.
The sharp, telling point of a joke or remark.
The compelling, insistent quality that makes something strike home.
To sharpen a statement so that its point stands out.
A formal spoken presentation to an audience.
The smooth, uninterrupted flow of spoken language.
To divide something into clear, ordered parts.
Expressed briefly and strikingly, with nothing wasted.
What is true and matches the facts.
A feeling of not being sure about something.
The power of the mind to think logically and judge soundly.
Awareness of oneself and the world; also the state of being awake.
The fact or manner of existing.
The way something is noticed or understood through the senses.
To explore something deeply until it is understood.
A trait of good moral character.
The idea that people can choose their actions freely.
Based on ideas rather than physical things; not directly representing reality.
The voice that tells a story.
A particular point of view; also the sense of depth in a picture.
A look back at earlier events; in a story, a scene from the past.
A recurring theme in a work; also a reason for acting.
Something that stands for or represents something else.
The quality of having more than one possible meaning.
To explain the meaning of something; also to point.
Figurative language that creates vivid pictures in the mind.
The level of formality or tone of a piece of language.
Open to several meanings.
The facts that make up an offence in law; the essential facts.
The body of decisions made by courts; the administration of justice.
To punish an offence officially.
The principles of right conduct in a society.
Able to be defended as reasonable or acceptable.
Responsibility for a wrong; also money that is owed.
Legal responsibility for damage or debt.
The mental capacity to be held responsible for one's acts.
To take responsibility and be accountable for something.
The power or right to give orders; also an expert.
A mild word used in place of a harsher or blunter one.
Information spread to promote a cause and shape opinion, often one-sidedly.
A model or framework of thought accepted in a field.
A claim put forward to oppose another.
The proving of a theory to be false through testing.
To check and confirm that something is true.
The extent of something's importance or consequences.
The state of not knowing what will happen.
For the time being; not yet final.
A fixed expression whose meaning is not literal.
A short, well-known saying that states a general truth.
A worn-out phrase used with little real meaning.
The style of language suited to a particular situation.
A secondary meaning or feeling that a word carries.
The informal language of everyday speech.
A fine shade of meaning, feeling, or tone.
A quality of feeling beneath what is openly said.
To adjust a colour or a meaning by a subtle degree.
Exactly right and well chosen.
The study of beauty and taste in art.
A quality of awe-inspiring grandeur beyond ordinary beauty.
A pleasing arrangement of parts; agreement and balance.
Art or objects seen as cheap, sentimental, and in poor taste.
The ideas or tastes shared by most ordinary people.
Artists whose work is experimental and ahead of its time.
The flavour of food; also a person's sense of style.
A judgement based on personal values rather than fact.
To recognise and honour the worth of something.
Showing good judgement of what is beautiful or fitting.
A demanding task that tests one's abilities.
A shortage of the materials needed to meet demand.
A profound and far-reaching change.
Able to remain workable and sustainable in the years ahead.
Something handed down by a predecessor; also property left in a will.
A duty that one is bound to fulfil.
The ability to judge wisely what the future may bring.
A description of a possible course of events.
An estimate of a whole based on a partial sample.
To predict something; also to choose not to do something.

id: voc:C2:den Nagel auf den Kopf treffen
def: To describe or identify something exactly right.
To buy something without checking it first.
To fail to see something plainly in front of one.
To say or do something socially clumsy.
To give up too soon in the face of difficulty.
To share private details or inside gossip.
To tell someone the plain, honest truth.
To be following a mistaken line of thought.
To look beyond one's own narrow point of view.
To wish someone good luck.
To irritate or annoy someone.
To admit defeat and give up.
A reply that answers or counters what was said.
A mistaken belief based on faulty reasoning.
The way evidence is presented to prove a point.
The quality of being sound and able to withstand scrutiny.
A sharpening of an issue to its critical point.
To weaken or disprove an argument.
To support a claim with evidence or argument.
To make something seem less absolute by weighing it against other factors.
To admit that something is true or valid; also to grant.
Following logically and leaving no doubt.
Sound and convincing, as a reason.
Strongly attacking or defending an opinion in a controversy.
The most essential part or core of something.
An idea or feeling a word suggests beyond its literal meaning.
Something suggested or logically entailed without being stated.
A division into two sharply opposed parts.
A contradiction between two apparently valid principles.
The relationship between cause and effect.
The quality of being possible but not necessary; dependence on chance.
The study of how language is used in texts and talk.
The theory and method of interpreting texts.
Reasoning that seeks truth through the clash of opposing ideas.
Knowledge based on observation and experience.
A practical method or rule of thumb for solving problems.
A traditional theme or standard formula in writing.
A story, or a way of framing events to convey a viewpoint.
A radical change that overturns an existing market or order.
The splitting of opinion into two strongly opposed groups.
The pushing of a group to the edges of society or attention.
